tools.description: List databases in the connected MongoDB instance.
tools.name: list_databases

tools.description: List databases in the connected MongoDB instance.
tools.name: list_databases

text:
Since the only databases shown are admin, config, and local, you’re looking at MongoDB’s system databases. What you do next depends on your goal. Here are practical options:

- Explore data (if you plan to add your own data)
  - In the shell:
    - use mydb
    - db.createCollection("mycollection")
    - db.mycollection.insertOne({ name: "Alice", age: 30 })
    - db.mycollection.find({})

- Inspect what's in the system databases
  - Admin users (in admin):
    - use admin
    - show collections
    - db.getUsers()
  - Config (in config):
    - use config
    - db.shards.find()
    - db.chunks.find({})  // if sharding is configured
  - Local (in local):
    - use local
    - db.oplog.rs.find().limit(5)  // only if you’re running a replica set

- Check server health and status
  - db.serverStatus()
  - db.stats()
  - If you’re on a replica set: rs.status() and rs.config()

- Set up authentication (recommended for production)
  - If auth isn’t enabled yet, you’ll typically:
    - create an admin user in admin
    - restart mongod with --auth
  - Example (in admin):
    - use admin
    - db.createUser({ user: "admin", pwd: "strongpassword", roles: [{ role: "root", db: "admin" }] })

- Back up or migrate
  - mongodump to back up
  - mongorestore to restore

Tell me your goal (explore data, configure security, monitor, backup, etc.) and your environment (shell, driver language, whether auth is enabled). I can give you exact commands tailored to you.
